item.title: No More Disruption: PlayStation Network’s Approaches To Avoid Outa... Tomoyuki Ehira & Shuhei Nagata
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
No More Disruption: PlayStation Network’s Approaches To Avoid Outages on Kubernetes Platform - Tomoyuki Ehira & Shuhei Nagata, Sony Interactive Entertainment
At PlayStation Network, our Kubernetes platform with 50+ clusters handles massive amounts of user traffic every day, and the platform team consists of engineers in several global locations with different technological and cultural backgrounds.
Despite such scale and organizational complexity, we achieved remarkable stability in FY2024 so far, maintaining a notable 99.995% uptime for our platform.
In this session, we will share the key practices behind this success, including a controlled deployment strategy, robust scaling techniques, minimized manual intervention, and 24/7 operations spanning global regions. While these approaches may not be special individually, their consistent and disciplined application has been the foundation of our platform's stability.
Those who strive to achieve stable platform operation and organizations looking to expand or consolidate their platforms will leave with actionable strategies to enhance the reliability of their platform.

item.title: New Cache Hierarchy for Container Images and OCI Artifact in Kub... Toru Komatsu & Hidehito Yabuuchi
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
New Cache Hierarchy for Container Images and OCI Artifact in Kubernetes Clusters Using Containerd - Toru Komatsu & Hidehito Yabuuchi, Preferred Networks, Inc.
One of the key bottlenecks in Kubernetes pod startup is the time taken to pull container images and OCI artifacts. It’s also costly to fetch large container images from the registry often. To tackle this problem, we developed a cache system with the following features:
* New Cache Hierarchy: Images pulled by pods are shared across the entire cluster, enabling cluster-wide optimization, not only cluster-local cache. * Ninja: Users experience faster container image pulls without any changes on their part. Just like a ninja, the system stealthily enhances performance. * Preheating: It supports pushing images to preheat the cache for subsequent pulls.
Deployed in a production cluster, the cache system has achieved a cache hit rate of around 95%, significantly reducing pod startup times and network communication with registries. Attendees will learn practical insights into leveraging cache and CRI to optimize image and OCI artifact pulls, ultimately enhancing cluster efficiency.

item.title: 2-Node Kubernetes: A Reliable and Compatible Solution - Xin Zhang & Guang Hu, Microsoft
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
2-Node Kubernetes: A Reliable and Compatible Solution - Xin Zhang & Guang Hu, Microsoft
High availability in Kubernetes typically requires a 3-node setup to support etcd's Raft algorithm. But what if you could achieve HA with only 2 nodes, slashing infrastructure costs by over 30% without sacrificing reliability? This is a game-changer, especially for deployments in retail and manufacturing scaling across hundreds or thousands of locations.
Join us to explore a groundbreaking 2-node HA Kubernetes solution, built by evolving the Raft algorithm for etcd. Unlike alternatives that compromise on compatibility, our approach delivers etcd-based HA, which can tolerate both node failures and network partitioning like a traditional 3-node cluster. You can seamlessly transit between 3-node and 2-node cluster utilizing standard tools like kubeadm or CAPI. This approach requires only a simple shared storage witness.
In this session, we will unpack the mechanics of this innovation, demonstrate 2-node cluster provisioning, and showcase its resilience under real-world failure scenarios.

item.title: Add Single-sign-on To Your Applications With Keycloak and Learn... Takashi Norimatsu & Marek Posolda
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Add Single-sign-on To Your Applications With Keycloak and Learn About Its Latest Features - Takashi Norimatsu, Hitachi & Marek Posolda, Red Hat
Keycloak is an Identity and Access Management (IAM) open-source software, and CNCF incubating project. Use it to add single-sign-on and authentication to your applications and secure your services with minimum effort.
In the first part of this talk, we will introduce Keycloak and tell what Keycloak can do, how you can use Keycloak, where Keycloak is used and how Keycloak can resolve the issue that developers encounter when using IAM. We'll look behind the scenes how Keycloak is managed by maintainers, how you can contribute to Keycloak, which community activities are going on, and how you can participate in the activities.
In the second part of this talk, we will explain the latest updates of Keycloak and introduce new features, enhancements of existing features. We will also describe planned features and enhancements for the future.

item.title: Breaking Limits: Highly-Isolated and Low-Overhead Wasm Container - Soichiro Ueda & Ai Nozaki
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Breaking Limits: Highly-Isolated and Low-Overhead Wasm Container - Soichiro Ueda, Kyoto University & Ai Nozaki, The University of Tokyo
Wasm is touted as the next generation of containers, offering a smaller, more secure, and more portable application format. However, challenges remain, particularly in achieving enough isolation for public clouds where multi-tenancy exists. This is because Wasm shares the host kernel between workloads like containers. To take full advantage of Wasm, there is still insufficient discussion on this problem.
To address the issue, we've developed a new Wasm runtime, Mewz. It runs a single Wasm module within a dedicated VM while also having a lightweight and specialized kernel (unikernel). This revolutionary execution model enables more secure and low-overhead Wasm containers. We've open-sourced the implementation, and Mewz is listed in the CNCF cloud native landscape! In this session, we'll explain the architecture of Mewz and why it's more isolated and low-overhead than ordinary Wasm runtimes. Building on this presentation, let’s discuss the future of cloud workloads powered by Wasm!

item.title: Streamlined Baremetal Deployment: A Journey of Custom Controll... Mitsuhiro Tanino & Masanori Kuroha
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Streamlined Baremetal Deployment: A Journey of Custom Controllers Integrated With OpenStack - Mitsuhiro Tanino & Masanori Kuroha, LY Corporation
As LY Corporation transitioned to developing a new private cloud infrastructure, we confronted significant challenges in managing over 6,000 baremetal servers through Kubernetes integrated with OpenStack, resulting in increased complexity, extended deployment times, and excessive resource consumption.
This session delivers how our custom controllers enhanced our approach, automating complex configurations and addressing disruptions in ArgoCD and Ansible, Kubernetes resource shortages, and scalability constraints.
We will focus on:
- Challenges in Existing Baremetal Provisioning: Explore the operational complexities and inefficiencies caused by scale, including deployment delays.
- Implementation of Custom Controllers: How we automated configurations and leading to faster, more reliable deployments with Helm.
- Enhancements in Resource Management: Techniques that streamlined processes and enhanced OpenStack integration, ultimately boosting operational simplicity and efficiency.

item.title: The Grand Adventure of Production Apps: Build, Break, and Survive! ~ A Kawaii Manga... Aoi Takahashi
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
The Grand Adventure of Production Apps: Build, Break, and Survive! ~ A Kawaii Manga Journey Through - Aoi Takahashi, Independent
"I've started to understand the basics of Kubernetes, but when it comes to running it in production, I can't quite imagine what kind of issues might arise..."
To ease these concerns, this session will use original characters, illustrations, and animations in a “cute manga” style to visually demonstrate how production applications can break and how to troubleshoot them. In our story, the main application as a character takes center stage as the hero, venturing out on a grand journey—only to be "suddenly attacked by a monster" at the most inopportune moment. By following this storyline, you will learn both how applications fail and how to fix them. Additionally, just as an adventurer equips better armor to prepare for future battles, we will explore common issues and explain how to prevent them from happening in the first place.
Join us on an exciting adventure in "manga-style troubleshooting" and gain the confidence to tackle production Kubernetes challenges head-on!

item.title: Never Underestimate Memory Architecture - Bryan Boreham, Grafana Labs
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Never Underestimate Memory Architecture - Bryan Boreham, Grafana Labs
Modern cloud servers are built on NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) and SMT (Symmetric Multi-Threading, aka Hyperthreading) — but few engineers realize how much these technologies impact application performance.
NUMA means that your cloud server’s memory might be significantly slower to access, because it’s connected to a different CPU, while Hyperthreading makes a single CPU core pretend to be two, but not at twice the speed.
This talk will: • Demystify NUMA & Hyperthreading — what they are, how they work, and why they matter. • Explore Kubernetes integration—the (limited) ways Kubernetes interacts with NUMA. • Show real-world performance impact — illustrated with measurements on AWS and Google Cloud. • Give you visibility — how to use Prometheus metrics and Linux commands to view your servers’ NUMA and SMT configurations.
By the end of the session, you'll have an understanding of the issues, and the tools to measure and their impact on the performance of your workloads.

Finally, the first KubeCon Japan is here, but it is an only start point. Japanese communities and companies are collaborating in CNCF Japan Chapter “Cloud Native Community Japan (CNCF)” to accelerate cloud native momentum in Japan and expand collaboration with other communities like FinOps Foundation and LF AI. In the talk, achievements of CNCJ since KubeDay Japan and forecast will be introduced.

item.title: Keynote: Unleashing AI Infrastructure and Platforms: Accelerating Innovatio... Sunyanan Choochotkaew
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Keynote: Unleashing AI Infrastructure and Platforms: Accelerating Innovation Through the Open Source Ecosystem - Sunyanan Choochotkaew, Staff Research Scientist, IBM Research
This session presents how open-source ecosystems can accelerate research and innovation in AI, highlighting the use of an AI-optimized, cloud-native supercomputing platform designed for the development and training of large-scale generative models. By leveraging cloud-native infrastructure and open-source technologies, the platform supports flexible model deployment, efficient orchestration, and rapid iteration—enabling continuous optimization. Within our research department, it plays a central role in several transformative AI initiatives, including the development of open-source large language models, and contributes to a range of open-source projects such as InstructLab, Data Prep Kit, MLBatch, Kqueue, PyTorch, vLLM, Kepler, SusQL, and Multi-NIC CNI.
With cloud-native technologies essential to maximizing these benefits, this highlights how active engagement with open source—not just as consumers, but as contributors—cultivates collaboration, unleashes AI infrastructure and platforms, drives sustainable innovation, and helps strengthen the broader community.

item.title: Safeguarding Your Applications - Achieving Zero Downtime During Kube... Kazuki Uchima & Kakeru Ishii
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Safeguarding Your Applications - Achieving Zero Downtime During Kubernetes Upgrades - Kazuki Uchima & Kakeru Ishii, Google Cloud
Kubernetes cluster upgrades are frequent and can lead to unforeseen problems, including application downtime. Therefore, it is essential to understand the roles of each component that makes up a Kubernetes cluster and how they behave during an upgrade in order to safely upgrade the Kubernetes cluster and achieve zero downtime for your applications.
This session will explain the basics and practical practices to improve safety, and ensure zero downtime for your applications. In 30 minutes, we will provide an easy-to-understand explanation of everything from the roles of the main Kubernetes components to points to note during upgrades and recommended configurations to minimize or eliminate application downtime during the upgrade process. This session is recommended for those who are going to operate Kubernetes in earnest and those who are troubled by upgrades and want to learn how to prevent application downtime during Kubernetes cluster upgrades.

item.title: The Future of Prometheus Exposition Format - Arthur Sens, Grafana Labs
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
The Future of Prometheus Exposition Format - Arthur Sens, Grafana Labs
OpenMetrics (OM) had a wild journey: it started as a project to standardize the Prometheus exposition format, and it became an entirely separate CNCF Incubating project. Even though the project had high maturity, it struggled for years to find tools to comply with the first version of the spec. Finally, in 2025, it was incorporated back into the Prometheus Github organization so Prometheus developers could lead the efforts for OM 2.0.
In this talk, Arthur, a Prometheus maintainer and OpenMetrics contributor, will walk you through the main challenges that tools like Prometheus and OpenTelemetry face when trying to comply with OpenMetrics 1.0 and how the community plans to address these challenges in OM 2.0.
The audience will also learn how changing an exposition format can make Prometheus and OpenTelemetry-Collector more memory-efficient while making their specifications easier to translate into each other!

item.title: Platform Engineering Day 2: Why Service Iterations Are the Crux of Developer Platfor... Puja Abbassi
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Platform Engineering Day 2: Why Service Iterations Are the Crux of Developer Platforms - Puja Abbassi, Giant Swarm
Everyone is talking about platform engineering. You see smooth demos of golden paths and self-service platforms. However, there’s a significant area of challenges that is less talked about and thus often neglected when designing developer platforms.
In this talk, we’ll explore the often-overlooked day 2 challenges that platform teams face. We’ll dissect the area of day 2 into the many sub-areas and challenges they pose. Drawing on real-world experiences, including notable migrations that many in this community have faced, we'll shed light on the pain behind developer platforms and discuss solutions to these issues. Among others, we’ll delve into practical strategies for managing versioning and rollouts, and highlight the significant hurdles encountered, such as dependencies on end user teams or GitOps.
Join us for insights, strategies, and stories from the trenches that will help you navigate the complexities of service iteration in developer platforms.

item.title: Keynote: Make Cloud-Native Ubiquitous: KubeEdge's Graduation Journey wit... Yue Bao & Hongbing Zhang
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Keynote: Make Cloud-Native Ubiquitous: KubeEdge's Graduation Journey with Innovation and Collaborative - Yue Bao, Huawei Cloud Computing Technology Co., Ltd. & Hongbing Zhang, DaoCloud
Sustainable growth of open-source projects requires both technological advancement and collaborative growth within a diverse community. How to create a community with vendor diversity that can collectively drive technological progress is a hot topic of concern today. It is increasingly clear that establishing the right governance structure and technical roadmap is critical during a project’s evolution.
KubeEdge, the industry’s first cloud-native edge computing open-source project, has grown from its launch in 2018 to achieving CNCF graduation this year. Over the past few years, KubeEdge has worked alongside multi-partners to implement practical applications in various fields, including satellite and smart vehicles, while fostering the robust development of multiple SIGs.
In this session, we will discuss the KubeEdge graduation journey, focusing on technical roadmap, community governance, and project maintenance. We will explore how to unite a diverse array of vendors within the community to advance our technological initiatives. Additionally, we will highlight the latest developments of KubeEdge in the fields of AI and robotics, aligning with current trends. Join us to explore how to build a mature, diverse, and technologically leading open-source community.

item.title: Secure Code Warrior Defines Security Rules for AI Coding
item.author: Mike Vizard

item.title: Demystifying— Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 4
item.author: Kiranms
item.content:encoded: Demystifying— Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 4So we have understand in our previous blog “Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 3” about Configure Cloudbees Jenkins Service to run with Service Account.So in this we are going to talk about “Part 4 :- Joining Cloudbees Jenkins Agent to CM & CM to Operation Center.” Lets us start with Joining Cloudbees Jenkins Agent to CM & CM to Operation Center. lets get into some technical action then…… 🏁🏁Before we start with Joining Cloudbees Jenkins Agent to CM & CM to Operation Center. You should know few things on Jenkins URL by-default firewall rules are not open to access , to do that you need to open the following ports to establish HTTP (8888) , HTTPS (8443) and Agent port (50000) connection to do so go through my HOW-TO blog at “How To — Open Firewall Ports in RHEL & CentOS Linux”Let us start with Joining Cloudbees Jenkins CM to Operation Center & then Jenkins agent to Jenkins CM.Before that lets us understand why do we need to join Controller to Operation Center. joining a Jenkins controller to a Jenkins Operation Center brings centralized management, scalability, high availability, global configuration management, reporting, and collaboration benefits. It provides us robust framework for managing and scaling Jenkins environments.Jenkins Operation Center acts as a central management point for multiple Jenkins instances. By joining a Jenkins controller to OC , we can centrally manage and monitor multiple Jenkins controllers and their associated jobs, plugins, configurations, and nodes from a single interface. OC allows us to scale our Jenkins environment efficiently. By joining a Jenkins controller to OC, we can easily add more Jenkins controllers and distribute the workload across. OC provides features like load balancing and distribution of jobs to ensure efficient resource utilization. OC enables high availability and fault tolerance for Jenkins controllers. we can set up fail-over mechanisms and automatic backups. If a Jenkins controller fails, OC can redirect the jobs to other available controllers and ensures continuous operation and minimize downtime.Global Configuration and Policy Enforcement: OC allows you to define global configurations and policies that can be enforced across multiple Jenkins controllers. OC provides advanced reporting and analytics capabilities for your Jenkins environment. We can access comprehensive data and metrics related to job execution, resource usage, build trends, and so on.Now lets us jump into to actual hands on by joining Cloudbees Operations Center to Jenkins Controller.There are two ways you can join your OC to Controller or from Controller to OC.Way 1 : — Join from Jenkins Operation Center to Jenkins Controller2. After clicking on “New Item” Enter item name of your controller that you want to connect to, in my case and then click on and then click on 3. After clicking on you will getting into the next page to enter some details enter your and click on and Note:- In this case we will just go over default setting.4. Then you will get into the next page for Pushing the connection details to controller that we want to connect. Please enter your URL of Controller and click on 5. Then you will be getting on the next page of “Join Operation Center Cluster” here you can see OC URL , Agent Address , Agent Port and Agent Status to online, then Click on 6. Now you can see Controller is connected with Operation Center successfully in below screenshot.Way 2: — Join from Jenkins Controller to Jenkins Operation Center.Open the Controller URL , In my case “http://jslaver8.cnl.com:8888/” and you will see screenPrompt there you will need click on “Join Cloudbees Operation Center”2. Once you click on “Join Cloudbees Operation Center” you will need to enter the connection details form your Operation Center.3. Go to Operation Center and Click on “New Item” Enter item name of your controller that you want to connect to, in my case and then click on and then click on 4. After clicking on you will getting into the next page to enter some details enter your and click on and Note:- In this case we will just going over default setting.5. Then you will get into the next page of Pushing the connection details and Connection Details from Operation Center. Copy those and paste in the Controller and click on 6. Here you will see we have join the Jenkins Controller to Operation Center successfully in other way.Configure Jenkins Agent with Controller:-Before we go ahead with configuring jenkins agent, let us understand what is Jenkins Agent.Jenkins Agents aka Jenkins Slave are worker nodes that perform tasks on behalf of the Jenkins Controller aka CM. Agents are responsible for executing build and deployment process, running test, and performing automated tasks. By distributing the workload to multiple agents, Jenkins enables parallel and distributed builds, improving efficiency and scalability. That is were Jenkins Agent comes into the picture.To configure Jenkins Agent with Controller you need to follow following steps.Login to controller in my case http://jslaver8.cnl.com:8888/ and go to and then click on and search for “SSH Agent Plugin” and then click on Install.2. Once Installed click on “Restart Jenkins when Installation is complete and no job are running” or once the plugins installed then enter URL like below add restart to restart the jenkins controller as below screenshot and click on http://jslaver8.cnl.com:8888/restart3. Once after Controller restart , login back to controller and click on or 4. Click on “New Node” and then enter “Node Name” in my case and click on radio button and click to proceed next.5. Then enter all the details accordingly with “Agent Name”, “Remote root directory” , “Labels” and select “Launch Method with Launch agent via SSH”.6. Then click on the credential of agent to connect with then click on “Kind” and select “SSH Username with Private Key” to connect as permanent agent. Then click on and enter private key in the dialog box with out space at the end and click on Add8. Then in the main screen you can see Jenkins Agent is connected successfully in the below screen shot.You can see that we have successfully connected jagentr8 to Jenkins controller in the picture.In the Left pane of you can see jagentr8 is connected and in idle state.You can see with same steps and process we have also joined and configure jagentr7 to Controller.Hope you are clear on How to join Cloudbees Jenkins Controller to Operation Center and Agent to Controller.if you like this blog please share, comment that motivates me to writes another great technological blogs.That’s all for this blog and will see you in next “Part 5:- Installing and Configure Maven, Ant and Gradle on both Cloudbees Jenkins Agents”

item.title: Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 5
item.author: Kiranms
item.content:encoded: Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 5So we have understand in our previous blog “Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 4” about Attaching Cloudbees Jenkins Agent to Controller and Controller to Cloudbees Jenkins Operation Center.So in this we are going to talk about “Part 5 :- Installing and Configure Maven, Ant and Gradle on Cloudbees Jenkins Agent & setup them on Cloudbees Jenkins Controller Web UI.Lets us start with Installing and Configure Maven, Ant and Gradle on both Cloudbees Jenkins Agentslets get into some technical action then…… 🏁🏁In this blog we are only going to talk about Maven, Ant, Gradle and Ansible there are many thing to configure on actual production grade Server, To avoid the length of the blog we are just talking about Maven, Ant , Gradle and Ansible.We are going to see all installation and configuration setup on RHEL8 Jenkins agent server only and Integration with Cloudbees Jenkins Controller.Note:- I am going to install all this tools in /opt for this demo series , you can install wherever you want or according to your organizational setupInstall Maven and configure in Jenkins Controller WebUIPlease copy/wget Maven package on the jagentr8 server for installation. In my case I have downloaded to my local system and then copy them over to agent.2. Then you need unzip Maven packages.#sudo unzip apache-maven-3.9.3-bin.zipunzip will create following directory with name.3. Move maven directory to maven.#sudo mv apache-maven-3.9.3 maven4. Create maven.sh in /etc/profile.d to setup environmental variables for Maven.#sudo vi /etc/profile.d/maven.shand enter the following variable details in the file and save.Once done then you need to load the environment variables in the current shell using the following command.#source /etc/profile.d/maven.sh5. Verify Maven installation.Let us integrate maven with Jenkins Controller to execute maven job on jenkins agent.Go to “Manage Plugins” and Install “Maven Integration plugin”.3. Then Click on “Manage Jenkins” and then Click on “Global Tool Configuration”4. Then go to “Maven” and Click on “Add Maven” then enter required details like “Name” and “MAVEN_HOME” which installed on jenkins agents and Click on Apply/Save.Install Ant and configure in Jenkins Controller WebUIUnzip Ant package in /opt.#sudo unzip apache-ant-1.10.13-bin.zip2. Move apache-ant-1.10.13 to ant#sudo mv apache-ant-1.10.13 ant3. Create ant.sh in /etc/profile.d to setup environmental variables for Ant.#sudo vim /etc/profile.d/ant.shand enter the following variable details in the file and save.Once done then you need to load the environment variables in the current shell using the following command.4. Verify Ant installation.Let us integrate ant with Jenkins Controller to execute ant on jenkins agent.Go to “Manage Plugins” and Install “Ant plugin” and Click on Install.2. Then Click on “Manage Jenkins” and then Click on “Global Tool Configuration”3. Then go to “Ant” and Click on “Add Ant” then enter required details like “Name” and “ANT_HOME” which installed on jenkins agents and Click on Apply and Save.Install Gradle and configure in Jenkins Controller WebUIUnzip Gradle package in /opt.#sudo unzip gradle-6.3-bin.zip2. Move gradle-6.3 to gradle#sudo mv gradle-6.3 gradle3. Create gradle.sh in /etc/profile.d to setup environmental variables for gradle.#sudo vim /etc/profile.d/gradle.shOnce done then you need to load the environment variables in the current shell using the following command.4. Verify gradle installation.Let us integrate Gradle with Jenkins Controller to execute Gradle jobs from jenkins agent.Go to “Manage Plugins” and Install “Gradle plugin” and Click on Install.2. Then Click on “Manage Jenkins” and then Click on “Global Tool Configuration”3. Then go to “Gradle” and Click on “Add Gradle” then enter required details like “Name” and “GRADLE_HOME” which installed on jenkins agents and Click on Apply and Save.Install Ansible and configure in Jenkins WebUIBefore you start installing Ansible you should have following requirement install on Ansible Master or Controller — Whatever you like to call 😉 😉Ansible Controller/Master :- This is the machine that runs Ansible, you can use nearly any UNIX-like machine which run Python 3.9 or newer.This is theachine that Ansible is managing and does not require Ansible to be installed, but requires Python 2.7, or Python 3.5–3.11 to run Ansible library code on the Ansible targets.Install Ansible.2. Verify Ansible Installation.Let us integrate Ansible with Jenkins Controller to execute Ansible Playbooks from jenkins agent.Login in to http://jslaver8.cnl.com:8888/ and go to “Manage Jenkins” and “Manage Plugins” search for Ansible and click on “Install without restart”2. Then go to “Manage Jenkins” and “Global Tool Configuration” you will see Ansible then provide the following details and “Save”. Note:- for “Path to ansible executable directory” you should provide the executable directory path where you have installed Ansible in my case I have installed on jagentr8 with /usr/bin/.We have configured and integrated Ansible with Jenkins Controller.Hope you are clear on How to Installing and Configure Maven, Ant , Gradle and Ansible on Cloudbees Jenkins Agent & setup them on Cloudbees Jenkins Controller Web UI.if you like this blog please share, comment that motivates me to writes another great technological blogs.That’s all for this blog and will see you in next “Part 6:- SSL Configuration on Cloudbees Jenkins Operation Center and Client-Master.”

item.title: How To — OpenSSL Overview
item.author: Kiranms
item.content:encoded: How To — OpenSSL OverviewSecure Sockets Layer (SSL) or often referred as Transport Layer Security (TLS). It is a standard technology used to establish a secure and encrypted connection between a web browser and a web server. SSL ensures that the data transferred between the browser and server remains private and cannot be Compromised or tampered with by hackers. It provides three fundamental layers of protection for our websites. SSL encrypts the data being exchanged, so even if intercepted, it appears as unreadable gibberish to anyone without the decryption key. This protects sensitive information like login credentials, credit card details, and personal data. SSL certificates verify the identity of the website. Which helps users trust that they are connecting to the intended website and not to malicious things on the internet. Valid SSL certificate shows that the website owner’s identity has authenticated by a trusted certificate authority (CA) which can be seen from the lock button from the URL path. SSL ensures that the data being transferred remains intact and un-compromised during transmission.Let me give you simple example why SSL is important when you are browsing secure and important Personal Information (PI) data. Let’s say Kiran wants to buy a product from an online site, and he is worried about the security of his PI data. Kiran wants to make sure that his Personal information does not expose and harm to his PI information.Let’s see what happens without SSL and with SSL online store sites.Kiran visit the online store’s website, and then he notices that the URL starts with (e.g., http://www.mystore.com).He add the product to cart and proceed to the checkout page.When he enters his credit card details and click “Submit,” the information is sent to the store’s server in plain text. Hackers or Malicious attackers read his PI data while it is transit and can compromise the information.Kiran visit the online store’s website, and he notices that the URL now starts with (e.g., https://www.mystore.com).He add the product to cart and proceed to the checkout page.When he enters his credit card details and click “Submit,” the information is encrypted before it is getting sent to the store’s server. This means that even if Hackers or Malicious attackers trying to exchange or compromise the data, they will only see a garbage characters. It is like sending his data in a locked box and only store’s server can unlock with is private key.He also notice a padlock icon in his browser’s address bar, indicating that the connection is secure. He can click on this icon to view the SSL certificate information, which includes details about the website’s identity and the organization that issued the certificate.OpenSSL is a widely used open-source software library that provides tools and libraries for Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) and Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocols. It primarily focuses on cryptographic functions and secure communication over computer networks and offers various purpose to manage SSL and TLS certificates.Here are several essential functions provided by OpenSSL toolsCertificate Signing RequestX.509 Certificate OperationsPublic Key Infrastructure (PKI) OperationsEncryption and DecryptionDigital Signature ManagementTypes of SSL Certificate ?SSL certificates come in various types and with different extensions. Lets looks at the most common types of SSL certificates and their associated file extensions.Domain Validated (DV) Certificate:Description: DV certificates are the most basic SSL certificates. They only validate the ownership of the domain and are typically the easiest and quickest to obtain. DV certificates are suitable for simple websites and blogs.2. Organization Validated (OV) Certificate:Description: OV certificates provide a higher level of validation than DV certificates. They verify the domain ownership and some basic organization information. These certificates are often used by small to medium-sized businesses.3. Extended Validation (EV) Certificate:Description: EV certificates offer the highest level of validation. They not only validate domain ownership and organization information but also require a more thorough verification process. Websites using EV certificates display a green address bar in most web browsers, signifying a higher level of trust.Description: Wildcard certificates secure a domain and its subdomains with a single certificate. For example, a wildcard certificate for can be used to secure mystore.com , sub.mystore.com, and any other subdomains. This simplifies certificate management for websites with many subdomains.5. Multi-Domain (SAN) Certificate:Description: Multi-Domain (Subject Alternative Name or SAN) certificates allow you to secure multiple domain names with a single certificate. This is useful for websites with multiple domains or subdomains. The certificate can include various domains or subdomains within a single certificate.6. Code Signing Certificate:Description: Code signing certificates are used to digitally sign software and applications to ensure their authenticity and integrity. These certificates are crucial for developers and software publishers.7. Email (S/MIME) Certificate:Description: Email certificates are used for securing email communication, specifically for digitally signing and encrypting emails. These certificates are essential for ensuring email authenticity and privacy.8. SSL/TLS Certificate Chain:Description: SSL/TLS certificate chains consist of multiple certificates, including the end-entity certificate, intermediate certificates, and root certificates. These are used to establish trust in the certificate hierarchy. This will not be a separate certificate type, they play a critical role in the SSL/TLS handshake process.Generate the private Key (RSA)# openssl genrsa -out myprivatekey.pem 20482. View Certificate Details#openssl rsa -text -in myprivatekey.pem -noout3. Extract Public Key from Private Key. #openssl rsa -in myprivatekey.key -pubout -out mydomain_public.key4. Creating CSR # openssl req -new -key myprivatekey.key -out mydomain.csr5. Verify CSR Information# openssl req -text -in mydomain.csr -noout -verify6. After sending your .csr to Certificate Authority , you will receive certificate .crt and run the following command to verify and match with our private key with x509 command.# openssl x509 -text -in mydomain.crt -nooutNote: I will be creating self sign certificate for my OC and Master configuration.This is one of the important task after you receive your key from Certificate Authority and validate the Private and Public key match with the hash output.#openssl pkey -pubout -in myprivatekey.key | openssl sha256#openssl req -pubkey -in mydomain.csr -noout | openssl sha256#openssl x509 -pubkey -in mydomain.crt -noout | openssl sha256 The above commands should be entered one by one to generate three separate outputs.8. Convert Certificate formats.#openssl pkcs12 -export -name “mydomain-key-(expiration date)” \-out mydomain.pfx -inkey mydomain.key -in mydomain.crtExtract the private key from a PKCS#12 (.pfx) file and convert it into a PEM#openssl pkcs12 -in mydomain.pfx -nocerts -out mydomain.key -nodesExtract the certificate from a PKCS#12 (.pfx) file and convert it into a PEM #openssl pkcs12 -in mydomain.pfx -nokeys -clcerts -out mydomain.crtHope you are clear on OpenSSL and some basic of Certificate Management part now.if you like this blog please share, comment that motivates me to writes another great technological blogs.

item.title: Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 6
item.author: Kiranms
item.content:encoded: Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 6So we have understand in our previous blog “Demystifying — Cloudbees Jenkins Migration — Part 5” about Installing and Configure Maven, Ant , Gradle and Ansible on Cloudbees Jenkins Agent & setup them on Cloudbees Jenkins Controller Web UI.So in this we are going to talk about “Part 6:- SSL Configuration on Cloudbees Jenkins Operation Center and Client-Master”Lets us start with SSL Configuration on Cloudbees Jenkins Operation Center and Client-Master or controller ,lets get into some technical action then…… 🏁🏁Before we start SSL configuration lets understand why do we need SSL configuration on Web sitesSecure Sockets Layer (SSL) or often referred as Transport Layer Security (TLS). It is a standard technology used to establish a secure and encrypted connection between a web browser and a web server. SSL ensures that the data transferred between the browser and server remains private and cannot be Compromised or tampered with by hackers. It provides three fundamental layers of protection for our websites. SSL encrypts the data being exchanged, so even if intercepted, it appears as unreadable gibberish to anyone without the decryption key. This protects sensitive information like login credentials, credit card details, and personal data. SSL certificates verify the identity of the website. Which helps users trust that they are connecting to the intended website and not to malicious things on the internet. Valid SSL certificate shows that the website owner’s identity has authenticated by a trusted certificate authority (CA) which can be seen from the lock button from the URL path. SSL ensures that the data being transferred remains intact and un-compromised during transmission.Lets start now with Jenkins SSL certificate configuration step by step. In this blog I will show you how to create self-signed SSL certificate and configure with self-signed certificate. You should have openssl installed in your Linux/Windows server to achieve SSL certificate tasks.Create a Private Key and a Self-Signed SSL CertificateGenerate a Server password key.#openssl genrsa -des3 -passout pass:changeit -out server.passwd.key 20482. Generate a private key server.key # openssl rsa -passin pass:changeit -in server.passwd.key -out server.key3. Delete the password server keyNote:- This steps is not mandatory4. Generate a server certificate and follow the prompt accordingly.# openssl req -new -key server.key -out server.csr5. Generate the SSL certificate server.crt#openssl x509 -req -days 365 -in server.csr -signkey server.key -out server.crt6. Create folder where you can keep all your generated certificate. For Demo purpose I am creating it under /var/lib/jenkins-oc/cert# mkdir -p /var/lib/jenkins-oc/cert# chown jenkins-user:jenkins cert# mv /home/jenkins-user/server.* /var/lib/jenkins-oc/cert/Configure Operations Center to use SSLGo to you OC configuration path and edit it to add JENKINS_ARGS.Edit your jenkins configuration and update following argument “JENKINS_HTTPS_PORT” and “JENKINS_ARGS”Note: we have already open the 8443 port in the filrewall , if you have not done this , do so.JENKINS_HTTPS_PORT="8443"JENKINS_ARGS="--httpsCertificate=/var/lib/jenkins-oc/cert/server.crt --httpsPrivateKey=/var/lib/jenkins-oc/cert/server.key --httpsPort=8443 --httpPort=-1"2. Once you have update the configuration for SSL , Please restart the Jenkins-oc service.#systemctl restart jenkins-oc.service3. Please open the https://jmasterr8.cnl.com:8443/ and see you will see your jenkins oc page is not secure. means we have configured SSL successfully just we need to import certificate details in Java cacert files. you can observ the https and 8443 port.4. Now we need to add our OC self-signed certificate to our controller keystore with keytool command.Note: We have to convert our .CRT to .CER certificate to import in cacerts.# mv server.crt server.cer# keytool -import -alias server -keystore /usr/lib/jvm/jdk-11-oracle-x64/lib/security/cacerts -file server.cer5. Add the following arguments to our OC JENKINS_JAVA_OPTIONS to-Djavax.net.ssl.keyStore=/usr/lib/jvm/jdk-11-oracle-x64/lib/security/cacerts -Djavax.net.ssl.keyStorePassword=changeit6. Restart your Jenkins OC to take in effects.# systemctl restart jenkins-oc.service7. Validate your SSL certificate for jmasterr8 has been configured successfully.So we have completed jmasterr8 Operation Centre configuration with SSL lets configure jslaver8 (http://jslaver8.cnl.com:8888/ ) Slave or CM to configure with SSL.Create cert directory to place the certificate at one location . you can create as you want.#mkdir -p /var/lib/cloudbees-core-cm/certGenerate a Server password key.#openssl genrsa -des3 -passout pass:changeit -out server.passwd.key 20483. Generate a private key server.key # openssl rsa -passin pass:changeit -in server.passwd.key -out server.key4. Delete the password server keyNote:- This steps is not mandatory5. Generate a server certificate and follow the prompt accordingly.# openssl req -new -key server.key -out server.csr6. Generate the SSL certificate server.crt#openssl x509 -req -days 365 -in server.csr -signkey server.key -out server.crtConfigure Client controllers to use SSLGo to your CM configuration path and edit it to add following JENKINS_ARGS.JENKINS_HTTPS_PORT="8443"JENKINS_ARGS="--httpsCertificate=/var/lib/cloudbees-core-cm/cert/server.crt --httpsPrivateKey=/var/lib/cloudbees-core-cm/cert/server.key --httpsPort=8443 --httpPort=-1"2. Once you have update the configuration for SSL , Please restart the Jenkins-cm service.#systemctl restart jenkins-oc.service3. Please open the https://jslaver8.cnl.com:8443/ and see you will see your jenkins oc page is not secure. means we have configured SSL successfully just we need to import certificate details in Java cacert files. you can observe the https and 8443 port.4. Now we need to add our CM self-signed certificate to our Slave keystore with keytool command.# mv server.crt server.cer# keytool -import -alias server -keystore /usr/lib/jvm/jdk-11-oracle-x64/lib/security/cacerts -file server.cer5. Add the following arguments to our OC JENKINS_JAVA_OPTIONS to-Djavax.net.ssl.keyStore=/usr/lib/jvm/jdk-11-oracle-x64/lib/security/cacerts -Djavax.net.ssl.keyStorePassword=changeit6. Restart your Jenkins CM to take in effects.# systemctl restart cloudbees-core-cm.service7. Validate you SSL certificate for jslaver8 has been configured successfully.Note — We are not going to cover SSL configuration for jmasterr7 and jslaver7 as it will be same process.We have successfully configured SSL configuration on Jmasterr8 OC and Jslaver8 CM.Hope you are clear on How to SSL Configuration on Cloudbees Jenkins Operation Center and Client-Masterif you like this blog please share, comment that motivates me to writes another great technological blogs.That’s all for this blog and will see you in next “Part 7:- Data Migrate from Jenkins jmasterr7 to jmasterr8.”
